attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic with a three-week history of loose, foul-smelling stools, bloating, and fatigue. She returned from backpacking in South-East Asia four weeks ago where she frequently drank untreated water. She has lost 3 kg since the onset of symptoms. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her abdomen is soft and mildly tender in the epigastrium.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
This patient presents with chronic diarrhoea, bloating, and weight loss following travel to South-East Asia with exposure to untreated water. The combination of symptoms lasting more than two weeks, foul-smelling stools, and significant risk factors makes giardiasis the most likely diagnosis.

Inflammatory bowel disease typically presents with bloody diarrhoea, abdominal pain, and systemic symptoms. While it can present at any age, the absence of blood in stools and clear temporal relationship to travel make this less likely.

Bacterial gastroenteritis typically causes acute symptoms with fever and resolves within 5-7 days. The chronic nature of symptoms and absence of fever make this diagnosis unlikely.

Coeliac disease can present with similar symptoms of chronic diarrhoea and weight loss. However, symptoms would not have such a clear relationship to travel, and patients typically report long-standing gastrointestinal symptoms with specific food triggers.

Irritable bowel syndrome can cause chronic diarrhoea and bloating but rarely causes weight loss. The clear temporal relationship to travel and exposure history make an infectious cause more likely.

Giardiasis is caused by the protozoan parasite Giardia lamblia and is commonly acquired through ingestion of contaminated water in endemic areas. The diagnosis can be confirmed with stool microscopy for ova, cysts and parasites, which would show characteristic oval-shaped cysts. Treatment typically involves single-dose tinidazole or a course of metronidazole.
learningPoints: Giardiasis typically presents with chronic diarrhoea, bloating, and fatigue in returned travellers or those exposed to contaminated water.
Stool microscopy for ova, cysts and parasites is the diagnostic test of choice for giardiasis.
Travel history and exposure to untreated water are key risk factors for giardiasis.
references: Giardiasis C, Chalmers R, Beeching NJ, et al. Giardiasis. BMJ 2016;355:i5369

attributes.name: Graduating
attributes.description: Only suitable for a graduating medical student at the end of their degree
